_about: Golden set for the AI poker coach (the dominant oracle). Five decisive-spot hands, one vs each distinct opponent type. Every hero_equity_pct is REAL, computed by playsim/playsim/equity.py (equity_mc, seed=42, samples=6000) -- not hand-authored. The decisive_opponent carries only the archetype; the style label, named leak, and tendency knobs are resolved from backend/coach/leaks.py at eval time (single source of truth, no drift). Each fixture's rubric is what a passing coaching response must satisfy.

hands.lesson: Value-bet bigger against a calling station; checking back a strong hand burns a clear value spot.

decisions.context: Heads-up vs the station on the river with an overpair; action checked to hero, hero checked back.

rubric.better_line: bet for value, sizing up because a station calls too wide

hands.lesson: Call down lighter against a maniac with a genuine bluff-catcher. An underpair that beats only his bluffs is a fold versus a disciplined player but a clear call versus someone who over-bluffs.

decisions.context: Hero has pocket nines -- an underpair to the king and queen on board, so it beats only bluffs and worse holdings. The maniac barreled all three streets and bet big on the river; hero folded. Pot odds need ~29%. The equity shown is vs a random hand.

rubric.better_line: call -- an underpair is a bluff-catcher here; against a maniac whose barreling range is bluff-heavy it is good well over the ~29% price, even though you would fold it to a disciplined player

hands.lesson: Bet to steal against a rock/nit that over-folds; checking back air gives up free equity they would have surrendered.

decisions.context: Hero has a backdoor-only hand (8-high) on an ace-king flop; the nit checked, hero checked back instead of taking a cheap stab.

rubric.better_line: bet as a semi-bluff/steal -- low equity is fine because a nit over-folds everything but a strong ace or king

hands.lesson: Don't bluff a solver-like player without equity or a real fold-out edge; they defend credibly, so a no-equity bluff just burns chips. (Solver-like is a style label, NOT a claim your line is optimal.)

decisions.context: Hero's gutshot (JT) missed; on the river hero fired a bluff into the solver-like player instead of giving up.

rubric.better_line: check / give up -- a busted hand with no fold-equity edge is -EV to bluff against a player who defends credibly

hands.lesson: DISCRIMINATING FIXTURE: fold to a grinder's three-street barrel even though your raw equity looks great. 68.8% is vs a RANDOM hand -- a disciplined grinder doesn't fire three barrels with random hands, so against the value-heavy range that actually bets here you're behind.

decisions.context: Hero's pocket tens face a grinder who bet flop, turn, and river on an ace-king board. Hero called the river. Pot odds need ~30%; raw equity is 68.8% vs a random hand.

rubric.better_line: fold -- against a grinder's three-barrel value range on an A-K board, tens beat only bluffs, so the 68.8%-vs-random number does not apply

hands.lesson: AFFIRMATION FIXTURE: the student played this correctly. Folding 7-high to a nit's turn raise is the right play -- the coach must CONFIRM it (verdict good), not invent a mistake or a 'better' line. Guards against the over-critical bias.

decisions.context: Hero's 7-high has no pair and no real draw on an A-K-9 board. A rock/nit raised the turn; hero folded. The equity shown is vs a random hand.

rubric.better_line: fold -- this is already the correct play; the coach should confirm it
